ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper studies a well-explored problem (unsupervised semantic relation representation using word embeddings) but contributes a notable empirical perspective: a large-scale experiment on ukWaC/SemEval data and the finding that second-order similarity and an input–output CBOW vector combination outperform common composition methods. Its main methodological novelty (using CBOW input/output vectors and analyzing why offsets work) is a non-trivial empirical extension rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. The work is therefore an incremental but useful advance that clearly differentiates its contributions from prior offset/addition/multiplicative baselines. | B(3): The paper addresses a well-studied problem (vector offsets and analogy-style relational similarity) and primarily contributes a large-scale empirical study and an input–output combination of CBOW vectors that outperforms other unsupervised composition methods. It clearly differentiates its findings from prior work (e.g., showing offsets arise from element-wise similarities and advocating second-order similarity) and reports thorough comparisons on SemEval and ukWaC. However, the techniques are largely empirical evaluations and combinations of existing vector-composition ideas rather than a fundamentally new methodology, so the novelty is moderate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper studies a well-explored problem (unsupervised semantic relation representation using word embeddings) but contributes a notable empirical perspective: a large-scale experiment on ukWaC/SemEval data and the finding that second-order similarity and an input–output CBOW vector combination outperform common composition methods. Its main methodological novelty (using CBOW input/output vectors and analyzing why offsets work) is a non-trivial empirical extension rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. The work is therefore an incremental but useful advance that clearly differentiates its contributions from prior offset/addition/multiplicative baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (vector offsets and analogy-style relational similarity) and primarily contributes a large-scale empirical study and an input–output combination of CBOW vectors that outperforms other unsupervised composition methods. It clearly differentiates its findings from prior work (e.g., showing offsets arise from element-wise similarities and advocating second-order similarity) and reports thorough comparisons on SemEval and ukWaC. However, the techniques are largely empirical evaluations and combinations of existing vector-composition ideas rather than a fundamentally new methodology, so the novelty is moderate.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses semantic relation representation using word embedding compositions, a central topic in NLP and representation learning and therefore squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses standard datasets (SemEval 2010 Task 8) and a large corpus (ukWaC), trains a CBOW word2vec model, and evaluates multiple vector-combination methods with clustering and a purity-based metric—methods and evaluations that the venue audience expects. The work is positioned with thorough related-work coverage and focuses on issues (unsupervised relation discovery, vector arithmetic explanations) that are of clear interest to the conference community. | B(5): The paper addresses semantic relation representation and unsupervised evaluation of word-embedding compositions—central topics in NLP/ML research—and therefore falls squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference. It targets the right audience by using standard resources and benchmarks (CBOW word2vec trained on ukWaC, SemEval 2010 Task 8) and compares multiple reasonable baselines (offset, difference, additive, multiplicative, concatenation) with clustering evaluation using a purity metric. The problem formulation (unsupervised relation clustering) and methods/evaluation (large-scale experiments, reported hyperparameters, comparative baselines) meet expectations for the venue, and the related-work coverage is thorough, indicating correct positioning within the research community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses semantic relation representation using word embedding compositions, a central topic in NLP and representation learning and therefore squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses standard datasets (SemEval 2010 Task 8) and a large corpus (ukWaC), trains a CBOW word2vec model, and evaluates multiple vector-combination methods with clustering and a purity-based metric—methods and evaluations that the venue audience expects. The work is positioned with thorough related-work coverage and focuses on issues (unsupervised relation discovery, vector arithmetic explanations) that are of clear interest to the conference community.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses semantic relation representation and unsupervised evaluation of word-embedding compositions—central topics in NLP/ML research—and therefore falls squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference. It targets the right audience by using standard resources and benchmarks (CBOW word2vec trained on ukWaC, SemEval 2010 Task 8) and compares multiple reasonable baselines (offset, difference, additive, multiplicative, concatenation) with clustering evaluation using a purity metric. The problem formulation (unsupervised relation clustering) and methods/evaluation (large-scale experiments, reported hyperparameters, comparative baselines) meet expectations for the venue, and the related-work coverage is thorough, indicating correct positioning within the research community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper ran large-scale unsupervised experiments comparing multiple vector-composition methods (addition, difference, concatenation, multiplication, input-output) on SemEval 2010 Task 8 using CBOW trained on ukWaC with reported hyperparameters and clustering evaluation, which provides solid empirical support for its main claims. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations/failure modes are discussed (lexical vs contextual issues, scaling problems with multiplicative methods, etc.). Missing, however, are explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses and some baseline details (an "unspecified baseline" is mentioned), and code is not reported, which slightly weakens reproducibility. | B(4): The paper runs a large-scale unsupervised experiment over ukWaC and SemEval 2010 Task 8, compares multiple composition methods (addition, concatenation, difference, multiplication, input-output) using clustering and modified purity, reports hyperparameters, and discusses negative results and limitations—supporting many of its claims. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying corpus, vector size, window, clustering settings) or multiple corpora/datasets to test generality, and the baseline clustering is only vaguely specified. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility may be limited by lack of released code.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper ran large-scale unsupervised experiments comparing multiple vector-composition methods (addition, difference, concatenation, multiplication, input-output) on SemEval 2010 Task 8 using CBOW trained on ukWaC with reported hyperparameters and clustering evaluation, which provides solid empirical support for its main claims. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations/failure modes are discussed (lexical vs contextual issues, scaling problems with multiplicative methods, etc.). Missing, however, are explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses and some baseline details (an "unspecified baseline" is mentioned), and code is not reported, which slightly weakens reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs a large-scale unsupervised experiment over ukWaC and SemEval 2010 Task 8, compares multiple composition methods (addition, concatenation, difference, multiplication, input-output) using clustering and modified purity, reports hyperparameters, and discusses negative results and limitations—supporting many of its claims. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying corpus, vector size, window, clustering settings) or multiple corpora/datasets to test generality, and the baseline clustering is only vaguely specified. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility may be limited by lack of released code.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its proposed input-output CBOW combination to a wide set of reasonable baselines (vector offset, vector difference, additive, concatenation, multiplicative, and a baseline clustering method) on the same SemEval 2010 Task 8 data using a consistent evaluation (modified purity) and reports hyperparameters. It explicitly acknowledges when prior approaches or the baseline perform comparably or better (e.g., baseline performs well; offset and multiplicative methods have clear shortcomings) and analyzes why differences arise. Related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor omission is that the unspecified baseline clustering method and lack of comparison to any recent supervised/deeper alternatives are not addressed, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(5): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of established baselines (vector offset, vector difference, additive, concatenation, multiplicative and a clustering baseline) and reports experiments on the same dataset (SemEval 2010) with a consistent clustering evaluation (modified purity). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform well or poorly (e.g. baseline clustering performs strongly; offsets and multiplicative methods have specific weaknesses). Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes differences and failure modes between the proposed input–output combination and the other vector-composition methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its proposed input-output CBOW combination to a wide set of reasonable baselines (vector offset, vector difference, additive, concatenation, multiplicative, and a baseline clustering method) on the same SemEval 2010 Task 8 data using a consistent evaluation (modified purity) and reports hyperparameters. It explicitly acknowledges when prior approaches or the baseline perform comparably or better (e.g., baseline performs well; offset and multiplicative methods have clear shortcomings) and analyzes why differences arise. Related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor omission is that the unspecified baseline clustering method and lack of comparison to any recent supervised/deeper alternatives are not addressed, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of established baselines (vector offset, vector difference, additive, concatenation, multiplicative and a clustering baseline) and reports experiments on the same dataset (SemEval 2010) with a consistent clustering evaluation (modified purity). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform well or poorly (e.g. baseline clustering performs strongly; offsets and multiplicative methods have specific weaknesses). Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes differences and failure modes between the proposed input–output combination and the other vector-composition methods.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear experimental flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion), and its main contributions are stated early and reinforced by thorough related work coverage. Notation and experimental details appear consistent and well-defined (hyperparameters reported, clear methodology using CBOW and ukWaC), and the writing quality is generally good. A minor presentation weakness is that some comparative details (e.g., the unspecified baseline) and visual/table support are not highlighted in the summary, so a few presentation elements could be clarified to reach excellence. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from introduction through method (detailed CBOW settings and evaluation protocol) to experiments and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and supported by large-scale experiments; notation and experimental details seem consistent (hyperparameters reported) and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation weaknesses are minor: the summary does not mention figures/tables or explicit descriptions of the baseline, so those elements may need clearer signaling, but overall the writing quality is reported as good.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a clear experimental flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion), and its main contributions are stated early and reinforced by thorough related work coverage. Notation and experimental details appear consistent and well-defined (hyperparameters reported, clear methodology using CBOW and ukWaC), and the writing quality is generally good. A minor presentation weakness is that some comparative details (e.g., the unspecified baseline) and visual/table support are not highlighted in the summary, so a few presentation elements could be clarified to reach excellence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from introduction through method (detailed CBOW settings and evaluation protocol) to experiments and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early and supported by large-scale experiments; notation and experimental details seem consistent (hyperparameters reported) and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation weaknesses are minor: the summary does not mention figures/tables or explicit descriptions of the baseline, so those elements may need clearer signaling, but overall the writing quality is reported as good.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are generally supported by the reported large-scale experiments (CBOW trained on ukWaC, evaluation via clustering SemEval 2010 Task 8 pairs) and the comparison of multiple composition methods, and hyperparameters and limitations are reported. There are no mathematical derivations to fault, so completeness/correctness of proofs is not applicable. The experimental methodology is largely sound (appropriate dataset, multiple baselines, clustering evaluation), but important details are missing or weakly justified in the summary—an unspecified but strong baseline, use of a modified purity metric without justification, and no mention of statistical significance testing. Overall the conclusions follow the presented results, though some claims (e.g., about inefficiency of offsets and scaling of multiplicative methods) would benefit from more rigorous statistical validation and clearer baseline description. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by large-scale unsupervised experiments (CBOW trained on ukWaC, vectors combined and clustered on SemEval 2010 Task 8) and report clear empirical comparisons across additive, difference, multiplicative, concatenation and an input–output CBOW combination. Experimental methodology is generally sound with reported hyperparameters and standard baselines, but the summary omits the definition of the unspecified strong baseline and any mention of statistical significance testing, which are minor weaknesses. No mathematical proofs are required by the work and none are claimed; overall the conclusions about relative strengths (e.g. input–output outperforming other combinations, offsets being unreliable at scale) are justified by the presented experimental evidence, though some claims (about why multiplicative methods fail or offsets lack constancy) would benefit from additional analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are generally supported by the reported large-scale experiments (CBOW trained on ukWaC, evaluation via clustering SemEval 2010 Task 8 pairs) and the comparison of multiple composition methods, and hyperparameters and limitations are reported. There are no mathematical derivations to fault, so completeness/correctness of proofs is not applicable. The experimental methodology is largely sound (appropriate dataset, multiple baselines, clustering evaluation), but important details are missing or weakly justified in the summary—an unspecified but strong baseline, use of a modified purity metric without justification, and no mention of statistical significance testing. Overall the conclusions follow the presented results, though some claims (e.g., about inefficiency of offsets and scaling of multiplicative methods) would benefit from more rigorous statistical validation and clearer baseline description.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by large-scale unsupervised experiments (CBOW trained on ukWaC, vectors combined and clustered on SemEval 2010 Task 8) and report clear empirical comparisons across additive, difference, multiplicative, concatenation and an input–output CBOW combination. Experimental methodology is generally sound with reported hyperparameters and standard baselines, but the summary omits the definition of the unspecified strong baseline and any mention of statistical significance testing, which are minor weaknesses. No mathematical proofs are required by the work and none are claimed; overall the conclusions about relative strengths (e.g. input–output outperforming other combinations, offsets being unreliable at scale) are justified by the presented experimental evidence, though some claims (about why multiplicative methods fail or offsets lack constancy) would benefit from additional analysis.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of representing semantic relations in vector spaces and provides a large-scale unsupervised evaluation (CBOW on ukWaC, clustering on SemEval 2010 Task 8) that clarifies why vector arithmetic analogies often work (due to individual element similarities) and shows that an input–output combination of CBOW vectors outperforms common composition methods in unsupervised clustering. These findings should change how researchers view offset-based methods and motivate further work on second-order similarity and input–output representations rather than relying solely on vector offsets. While the unsupervised setting and noted limitations (lexical vs. contextual instances, strong baseline) may limit immediate widespread application, the results open clear, significant research directions and are likely to influence future work in relation representation and composition.} PMID: Not applicable. Invalid trailing text.} Invalid trailing text.}  | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of representing and clustering semantic relations and does so with a large-scale unsupervised experiment (ukWaC, SemEval 2010 Task 8) that yields actionable insights. Demonstrating that much of analogy-vector arithmetic success stems from individual-element similarities and that an input–output CBOW combination outperforms other composition methods in almost every unsupervised clustering setting is likely to change how researchers design relation representations. The findings (e.g., limits of offsets and multiplicative methods, value of second-order similarity) open clear follow-up directions such as hybrid methods and relation-aware clustering. These contributions are broadly useful to the semantic-relation and vector-space communities and are likely to be built upon, though they stop short of being transformative across all of NLP.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of representing semantic relations in vector spaces and provides a large-scale unsupervised evaluation (CBOW on ukWaC, clustering on SemEval 2010 Task 8) that clarifies why vector arithmetic analogies often work (due to individual element similarities) and shows that an input–output combination of CBOW vectors outperforms common composition methods in unsupervised clustering. These findings should change how researchers view offset-based methods and motivate further work on second-order similarity and input–output representations rather than relying solely on vector offsets. While the unsupervised setting and noted limitations (lexical vs. contextual instances, strong baseline) may limit immediate widespread application, the results open clear, significant research directions and are likely to influence future work in relation representation and composition.} PMID: Not applicable. reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of representing and clustering semantic relations and does so with a large-scale unsupervised experiment (ukWaC, SemEval 2010 Task 8) that yields actionable insights. Demonstrating that much of analogy-vector arithmetic success stems from individual-element similarities and that an input–output CBOW combination outperforms other composition methods in almost every unsupervised clustering setting is likely to change how researchers design relation representations. The findings (e.g., limits of offsets and multiplicative methods, value of second-order similarity) open clear follow-up directions such as hybrid methods and relation-aware clustering. These contributions are broadly useful to the semantic-relation and vector-space communities and are likely to be built upon, though they stop short of being transformative across all of NLP.

main_claims: Presented an unsupervised large-scale experiment to identify relational similarities in word embedding compositions.
Confirmed that much of the success of vector arithmetic for analogies comes from similarities between individual elements.
Taking second-order similarity into account improves relational similarity and helps represent entity pairs for semantic relations.
Proposed an input-output combination of CBOW vectors that outperformed other vector combination methods in almost every unsupervised clustering setting.
_summary.methodology: Trained a word2vec CBOW model (negative sampling, window size 10, vector size 400) on the ukWaC corpus, extracted input and output vectors and evaluated several vector-combination representations (addition, concatenation, difference, multiplication, input-output combination) by clustering pairs from the SemEval 2010 Task 8 relation dataset using Cluto and measuring cluster quality with a modified purity metric.
baselines: vector offset method (b2 ≈ b1 − a1 + a2)
baseline clustering (unspecified baseline that performs well)
limitations: Unsupervised relation classification is very challenging due to lexical vs. contextual relation instances and differing semantic constraints on relation arguments.
Vector offset method is the least efficient at capturing generic semantic relations at large scale.
Vector difference representation does not conserve pairwise similarities and offsets are not constant enough for unsupervised clustering.
Multiplicative methods do not scale well and lose precision from individual similarities.
Baseline performs well and is difficult to enrich with relation-aware information.
Future work needs hybrid solutions separating relations with semantically constrained arguments from free ones and adapting clustering to handle outliers.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic relation classification / vector space semantics
